data.title: Ossoff reelection more likely: Sabato's Crystal Ball
data.description:
A nonpartisan election handicapper shifted its rating of Sen. Jon Ossoff’s (D-Ga.) reelection bid from a “toss-up” to “leans Democratic” in a new analysis on Thursday. 

Kyle Kondik, managing editor of Sabato’s Crystal Ball from the Center for Politics at the University of Virginia, wrote in his analysis that Democrats have previously been able to elect members of their party to the Senate from Georgia during the 2020 and 2022 election cycles and “they should be favored to do so in a 2026 midterm, too, particularly when the GOP is not guaranteed to produce a very strong challenger.” 

Kondik noted that Sen. Raphael Warnock (D-Ga.) had been able to win a first full term despite running under a Democratic president during a midterm election, while also pointing out that Republican candidate Herschel Walker was an exceptionally compromised candidate.

“The GOP nominee probably won’t have Walker’s baggage but they also probably won’t have his name ID, either,” Kondik said. 

The Sabato’s Crystal Ball manager editor, however, suggested that Ossoff still had some challenges, including running in a pivotal swing state, and there was an open question of whether he’d be able to turn out enough Black voters for his reelection. 

“This race is likely to be decided by only a few points either way — we just think Ossoff is likelier to end up on the winning side of that margin,” Kondik added. 

Ossoff is vying for reelection against three Republicans: Reps. Buddy Carter and Mike Collins, in addition to former University of Tennessee football coach Derek Dooley. Dooley is backed by Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp (R), Carter has the ability to self-finance some of his campaign and early public polling shows Collins as an early frontrunner. 

However, Collins’s campaign is also contending with a House Ethics Committee review of allegations regarding the congressman’s former chief of staff that could impact the contours of the race. 

Ossoff is the only Senate Democrat running for reelection this cycle in a battleground state Trump won in 2024 and is seen as key pickup opportunity for Republicans. However, the GOP primary has yet to solidify and coalesce behind a candidate ahead of the May primary, putting pressure on Republicans to narrow the field soon.

data.title: Trump administration: San Jose State broke law by allowing transgender volleyball player
data.description:
The Education Department said Wednesday that San José State University violated Title IX by allowing a transgender woman on its women’s volleyball team.  

The department began its investigation last February, concluding the policies allowing the transgender athlete into female-only facilities violated Title IX, along with alleged retaliation against teammates who opposed the policies. 

“SJSU caused significant harm to female athletes by allowing a male to compete on the women’s volleyball team — creating unfairness in competition, compromising safety, and denying women equal opportunities in athletics, including scholarships and playing time. Even worse, when female athletes spoke out, SJSU retaliated — ignoring sex-discrimination claims while subjecting one female SJSU athlete to a Title IX complaint for allegedly ‘misgendering’ the male athlete competing on a women’s team. This is unacceptable,” said Assistant Secretary for Civil Rights Kimberly Richey.  

“We will not relent until SJSU is held to account for these abuses and commits to upholding Title IX to protect future athletes from the same indignities,” Richey added. 

The proposed resolution agreement the department is offering to resolve the violation includes San José State agreeing not to let transgender athletes on women’s sports teams, sending a personal apology to the women who were on the volleyball team during this time and changing the athletic records to not include the transgender athlete’s scores. 

The Hill has reached out to the university for comment.  

If it does not agree to the resolution agreement, the Trump administration could attempt to pull its federal funding. 

San José State is the latest in a series of institutions the Trump administration has gone after for policies regarding transgender athletes.  

It also comes as the Supreme Court is set to rule this summer on the legality of transgender athlete bans in schools.

data.title: Argentina pays its debt: The US-Latin America strategy becomes clearer
data.description:
After it came to light last October that the U.S. would extend a $20 billion currency swap line to Argentina — a lifeline to stabilize the peso as pre-election panic threatened to unravel Javier Milei’s reforms — President Trump’s critics on both the left and the isolationist right wasted precious little time decrying the move. 

Sen Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) blasted Trump for “putting himself and his billionaire buddies first and sticking Americans with the bill,” going so far as to introduce legislation to block the credit line altogether.

Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) piled on that the package was “probably one of the grossest things” that the administration had done. Both sides warned that American taxpayers would never see a dime of it again — that Argentina’s long history of defaults made repayment a fantasy.

They were all wrong. 

Earlier this month, Argentina repaid the $2.5 billion it had taken from the package, with interest and ahead of schedule. The swap line worked exactly as Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent had intended: as a short-term bridge to support an ally during acute market stress. This was never going to be a bailout.

When Milei assumed Argentina’s presidency in December 2023, he inherited an economy virtually everyone agreed was beyond saving. Annual inflation was climbing month over month before peaking at 289 percent. Poverty exceeded 40 percent. The central bank was essentially out of cash, and the only thing the Casa Rosada had come up with in decades was to print more pesos.

Milei’s candidacy was deeply controversial, both domestically and internationally. Outlets that really should have known better — The Economist first and foremost — ran articles warning that he could spell an end to the nation’s democracy and decrying his alleged “dangerous allure.” Over 100 economists signed a letter predicting that his proposed reforms would spell “devastation” for his country.

It didn’t turn out that way, though. So far, annual inflation has fallen to 31.4 percent — still far too high, but enormous progress nonetheless. Over the same time, Argentina has posted its first budget surplus in 14 years, and the S&P has upgraded the country’s credit rating for the first time in years.

Bessent’s bet on Argentina wasn’t charity, but rather strategy. China has spent two decades building relations and cultivating influence across Latin America, largely while Washington remained distracted elsewhere. Typically, the view of the region was that it was something to be managed when things got really out of hand, but there was never any coherent idea about stabilizing the region, much less developing it. 

That has been changing over the last few years. Voters across Latin America have been throwing out failed left-wing governments and electing reformers who can deliver competent governance instead.

Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele has transformed his country from the murder capital of the world into a place safer than Canada. Bolivia has recently ended nearly two decades of socialist governance. Chile has just elected a staunch conservative on a broadly reformist, law-and-order platform.

These countries have moved decisively in a pro-American direction at precisely the same time that traditional Atlanticist alliances have begun to fray. Germany’s Friedrich Merz has openly urged Europe to “achieve independence” from the U.S., while tensions over trade and defense spending continue to mount.

Latin America is leaning in, just as Western Europe pulls away. Bessent has called the current window to make allies in South America a “generational opportunity.” 

One fundamental misunderstanding that many have is the view that the last century of stagnation in Latin America is destiny for future performance. Quite the contrary, the region’s stagnation will be looked back upon as a lost century. 

In the early 1900s, Argentina was richer per capita than France or Germany. It took decades of mismanagement to squander that inheritance. Others, like Venezuela, were some of the richest countries on earth until socialist governments were elected, sealing their fate for decades. 

The Argentina loan shows that this pivot is both effective and advantageous to the U.S. American support flowed to a genuine reformer at a critical moment, costing us nothing while generating tens of millions in profit, stabilizing a crisis before it could spiral — all while strengthening ties with a government that sits on one of the world’s largest lithium reserves. 

Compare that to the billions we have poured into allies who lecture us about values while free-riding on American security guarantees, and I think you’ll see the wisdom in the administration’s pivot.

Kyle Moran is a senior contributor with Young Voices, specializing in international affairs and national security.

data.title: 51 percent in Virginia poll favor redistricting
data.description:
A narrow majority of Virginia voters support mid-cycle redistricting, new polling shows, as Democrats in the state work to put forward an amendment this spring.

The Wason Center for Civic Leadership at Christopher Newport University found 51 percent of Virginia voters backed a constitutional amendment that would let the state legislature temporarily draw new district lines outside of the usual Census-tied timeline.

Another 43 percent disapproved of the plan and seven percent weren’t sure. While 61 percent of Republicans opposed the proposal, 63 percent of Democrats supported it, according to the poll.

After state lawmakers passed the proposal this month, a local county circuit court judge ruled Tuesday that Virginia Democrats would not be allowed to move forward with the amendment, which was set to go before voters in April.

Democrats appealed on Wednesday, projecting confidence that the measure would move forward. 

The Virginia push is the latest in an ongoing redistricting fight across the country, as Democrat-led states seek to offset GOP-friendly redistricting encouraged by President Trump over the last year. Meanwhile, Republicans in Florida are eyeing mid-decade redistricting as control of Congress hangs in the balance of November’s midterm elections.

Despite the majority of Virginia voters who said they support the proposed amendment, 63 approve of the way Virginia currently handles redistricting, the survey found.

The poll was conducted Jan. 13-20 among 807 registered Virginia voters and has a margin of error of plus or minus 4.4 percentage points.

data.title: Trump's Arctic imperialism risks global climate meltdown
data.description:
President Trump’s obsession with “owning” Greenland has been described as a massive threat to global security that undermines NATO and the trust of our closest allies in Europe, who have responded by sending additional troops to Greenland. Trump’s on-again, off-again intention to impose still higher tariffs on major European Union countries over the issue also reignited a trade war with Europe, putting the global economy and U.S. consumers at risk. 

But lost among these dangers is the most basic one: that Trump’s energy and climate policies, including his heedless preoccupation with exploiting Greenland and the rest of the Arctic for oil and gas resources, risk a far more rapid meltdown of the Arctic, with disastrous consequences for nations and people around the world.

The Arctic region, including Greenland, is already warming four times faster than the rest of the planet, and is tremendously vulnerable to even more rapid warming from additional greenhouse gas emissions. Limiting Arctic temperatures by just a few tenths of a degree turns out to be crucial to preventing temperature increases from spinning out of control worldwide. 

More than half of the Arctic’s reflective ice has melted in the last 50 years, and a recent study in the journal Nature found that the Arctic will be free of sea ice entirely for at least a day before 2030. Should Arctic sea ice be allowed to melt, which may happen within just two decades or even sooner, absorption of the sun’s heat by the newly open northern ocean will add the equivalent of 25 years of world-wide carbon dioxide emissions, pushing already dangerous global temperatures of 2.7 degrees Fahrenheit above preindustrial levels toward climatic instability.  

In fact, this loss of Arctic sea ice is just one of more than a dozen temperature-sensitive tipping points scientists have now identified, including in ocean currents and the Amazon rainforest, that risk unleashing super-heating around the globe. Such tipping points will move natural systems from net warming inhibitors, as they are today, toward natural systems actually forcing temperature increases or releasing greenhouse gases themselves.

In this context, preventing further loss of Arctic Sea ice is especially critical, as preventing it can forestall a domino effect of climate change tipping points in many other natural systems.  

For example, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration recently issued a study showing that increased warming and wildfires are now causing the Arctic tundra to emit more carbon than it stores, another tipping point already further speeding the cycle of Arctic melting. And new data just this month shows that Arctic sea ice has reached a record low for mid-January this year, with more than 2 million square kilometers less ice today than in the 1980s.  

For all of these reasons, Greenland instituted a moratorium on oil and gas exploitation in 2021. But even more effective actions must be taken to limit Arctic warming, especially the mitigation of super pollutants like methane.  

Reducing methane and other pollutants like hydrofluorocarbons can avoid nearly half a degree Celsius of warming by 2050 — four times more than from reducing carbon dioxide alone. In contrast, reducing carbon dioxide would only limit temperatures by one-tenth of a degree over the next 25 years, although it is still necessary to limit long-term temperatures.

So far, public policy discussions and media coverage have focused almost exclusively on the narrow economic opportunities that an ice-free Arctic might provide for shipping and resource development. But huge new shipping traffic in the Arctic and industrial development of oil and gas in the region will greatly increase the amount of climate pollution, including from carbon dioxide, methane and especially black carbon soot, which is already washing out onto Arctic ice and increasing melting rates tremendously.  

Such policies would turn the Arctic into yet another industrial region with massive new emissions at just at the moment Arctic temperatures desperately need to be contained. 

Unfortunately, Vladimir Putin also has grand plans for Arctic oil and gas, since the Russian Arctic contains large oil and gas reserves. Trump has used Putin’s Arctic ambitions as the supposed reason why the U.S. should illegally occupy Greenland. But hypocritically, the Trump administration and Putin have already begun meeting to discuss joint attempts to develop additional Arctic oil and gas.  

Along with oil and gas, Greenland contains significant mineral resources that can be of value to advanced technologies, such as electric vehicles, renewable energy and battery storage. Yet Trump has eliminated all incentives for the U.S. to take a leadership position in the manufacture of clean energy technologies, going so far as to stop renewable energy projects that have already received tens of billions in private investments. Indeed, Trump’s policies have allowed China to dominate manufacturing of lucrative and strategic clean energy technologies, controlling as much as 70 percent of global production.  

Meanwhile, U.S. greenhouse gas emissions, which had been falling for more than two decades, are now rising again under Trump, in large part due to him overruling private-sector preference to close coal-fired power plants. Trump has also prevented the implementation of key methane regulations, repealed major carbon dioxide standards, and withdrawn the U.S. from major climate change agreements, even though every other nation supports them.  

Trump’s anti-climate policies have already been damaging to the Arctic and global climate protection. Now his deeply misguided plans for Greenland and the rest of the Arctic, if allowed to occur, would increase the chances of disastrous, runaway climate change, threatening the safety and quality of life not only of the people of the world’s far North, but communities and people around the world. 

Paul Bledsoe is professorial lecturer at American University’s Center for Environmental Policy.  He served at the U.S. Interior Department and the White House Climate Change Task Force under President Clinton.

data.title: Car rams into Chabad headquarters in New York City
data.description:
A man is in custody after driving his car Wednesday night into the world headquarters of the Chabad-Lubavitch movement in New York.

New York Police Department (NYPD) Commissioner Jessica Tisch said Wednesday at a press conference that officers observed a vehicle strike the rear door of the building, located in the Crown Heights neighborhood of Brooklyn, at roughly 8:46 p.m. EST. Tisch added that the car then reversed and struck the rear door a second time.

Video of the incident shared to social platform X by Motti Seligson, director of media at Chabad, shows a dark-colored car driving into the building, then backing up slowly. 

Chabad, an Orthodox Jewish movement, is the largest and fastest growing Jewish organization in the world, with 3,500 institutions in 100 countries and territories, according to its website.

Tisch added that no injuries resulted from the crash, and no explosives were found in the car. The incident is being investigated as a hate crime and the NYPD has “significantly increased” its presence around houses of worship throughout the city, she said. 

New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) said her office will work with law enforcement to investigate the incident.

Seligson wrote on X that the organization’s headquarters “is perhaps the most replicated building in the world because of the light, joy, Torah and a confident Judaism that emanates from it to all corners of the world.” He noted that the driver’s motivation is unclear.

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani (D) called the incident “deeply alarming” and said that “any threat to a Jewish institution or place of worship must be taken seriously.”

“Antisemitism has no place in our city, and violence or intimidation against Jewish New Yorkers in unacceptable,” he told reporters Wednesday evening. “I stand in solidarity with the Crown Heights Jewish community, and I am grateful to our first responders for taking swift action.”

The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) of New York and New Jersey wrote on Facebook Wednesday that its “deeply disturbed” by the incident and is in touch with law enforcement and local partners on the ground.

“This building is not only a synagogue, but also the worldwide [Chabad] Headquarters and a beloved symbol of Judaism around the world,” the ADL added.

data.title: Schumer, Trump working on deal to avoid government shutdown
data.description:
Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) and President Trump are moving toward a deal to separate Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding from five other House-passed appropriations bills in hopes of avoiding a partial government shutdown this weekend, according to a source familiar with the discussions.

No agreement has been reached yet but the discussions are ongoing and moving in the Democrats’ direction, according to the source.

Schumer and Senate Democrats are demanding that the pending DHS funding bill be pulled out of the six-bill appropriations package, which the upper chamber is scheduled to vote on Thursday.

The Democratic leader wants to pass a short-term extension of funding for the agency to give Congress time to negotiate and pass legislation to overhaul Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Customs and Border Protection (CBP) to “rein in ICE and the violence.”

“This is not border security, this is not law and order, this is chaos — created at the top and felt in so many of our neighborhoods,” Schumer said at a press conference Wednesday.

The New York senator has laid out three major demands for reforming ICE and other federal law enforcement agencies working on immigration enforcement.

Democrats want to end roving patrols by ICE officers and tighten rules governing the use of warrants, establish a code of conduct for federal law enforcement officers’ use of force, prohibit officers from wearing masks and require them to wear body cameras and proper identification.

Some Republican senators are willing to split off the Homeland Security appropriations bill from the six-bill funding package that passed by the House last week.

GOP members of the Appropriations Committee want to get the Departments of Defense, Health and Human Services, Transportation and others funded before the Friday deadline, even if it means having to swallow a stopgap funding measure for Homeland Security.  

“What are our choices? To throw out all the bills? Or one?” said Sen. John Kennedy (R-La.), a member of the Appropriations panel. “I think a rational person would say let’s pass what we can and work on the others.”

Republican sources say that the fate of the funding package will hinge on Schumer’s negotiations with the White House.

“I think right now the conversation should be between the White House and Democrats,” Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) told reporters Wednesday.

“The White House obviously is open to negotiation,” he added.

data.title: Trump backed off his Greenland threats, but markets still aren't convinced
data.description:
Speeches by political and business leaders at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland typically are forgotten after they are delivered. Most are ignored by investors. 

But not this time. The reason: Observers were fixated about what President Trump would say following his threats to acquire Greenland either through purchase or military means. Previously, he had also announced that eight European countries would face tariff increases of 10 percent if they did not back him.

Trump began by reassuring the Davos gathering that the U.S. would not invade Greenland. However, he also warned NATO members that he would remember if they did not allow the U.S. to acquire the “piece of ice,” which he considered a small ask in exchange for all the U.S. had done.

The big surprise occurred when Trump announced the framework of a future deal involving Greenland had been reached with NATO General Secretary Mark Rutte, and his threat to impose tariffs would be rescinded.

This reversal has caused observers to speculate about why Trump retreated so quickly. A common explanation is that it is just the latest example of Trump making strong demands in negotiations and then chickening out.

The main reason, however, is that he encountered stiff resistance from European leaders after they had tried to appease him previously. This became evident when they met ahead of Davos to discuss whether to invoke the European Union’s Anti-Corruption “trade bazooka,” which would have revived the trade war between the U.S. and EU after an August truce that was highly favorable to the U.S.

European leaders took a firm stance this time for two reasons. First, they were outraged that Trump was not dealing with them in good faith. Second, they believed he was undermining the shared values of Western democracies throughout the post-war era.  

The highlight for many observers was the address delivered by Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney. He talked about the “rupture in the world order” and the beginning of “a brutal reality” where geopolitics among great powers is not subject to any constraints.   

The breakdown in the post-war order began with Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcement in April, when he replaced the international trading system based on the principle of most favored nation with “reciprocal tariffs.” While this action was motivated by economic considerations, strains between the U.S. and other members of the Western alliance subsequently increased over national security issues. 

European leaders were willing to make trade concessions to the U.S. in return for its support of Ukraine. However, they took a stand when Trump threatened the sovereignty of a NATO member and attempted to hold them ransom. 

So, what impact might this development have on the willingness of global investors to hold U.S. financial assets and the dollar? Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent’s view is they should ignore it on grounds that nothing has changed fundamentally, and America’s allies still are dependent on U.S. protection. 

Nonetheless, the big unknown is how long markets will stay calm in the face of growing geopolitical uncertainty. In this regard, there are several indications of growing unease.   

One is the steady surge in prices for gold and silver to record highs and renewed pressure on the U.S. dollar recently. It sank to a four year low this week after Trump said he was not concerned about the slide. 

Another is evidence that trading partners of the U.S. are diversifying their exports away from the U.S. to Asia, Latin America and other regions. Canada is also in the process of securing a trade deal with China, for which President Trump has threatened to impose 100 percent tariffs on it. 

Meanwhile, some observers have speculated about Europe “weaponizing” its sizable holdings of U.S. stocks and bonds. The likelihood of mass selling of U.S. securities is low, however, as most of them are held by private funds.  

That said, international equities have outperformed U.S. stocks over the past year following a prolonged period in which they underperformed. One sector that has produced stellar returns is defense stocks. A Bloomberg article notes that a Goldman Sachs basket of European defense stocks is off to a strong start in 2026 following a 90 percent advance in 2025.    

Amid this, investors need to consider what a weakening of U.S. leadership would mean for the world. 

In his book about the Great Depression, Charles Kindleberger argued that the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act was a deflationary force, but the real failure that occurred then was the lack of international leadership. He wrote: “The signing of the bill was a ‘turning point in world history,’ not for technical reasons, but because it made clear that in the world economy no one was in charge.” 

Kindleberger concluded that if the United States did not take a leadership role, “nothing happened.” In reflecting on this, I am left wondering, will this be true again?   

Nicholas Sargen, Ph.D., is an economic consultant and is affiliated with the University of Virginia’s Darden School of Business. The second edition of his book, “Global Shocks,” is scheduled for release in March.

data.title: Live updates: Homan addresses Minneapolis situation; Senate tries to keep government open
data.description:
Trump administration border czar Tom Homan will hold a press conference Thursday morning as he assumes command of the immigration crackdown in Minneapolis.

The Senate is also scrambling as a Jan. 30 partial shutdown deadline quickly approaches and Democrats dig their heels in on Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding.

After Saturday’s fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by immigration officials in Minneapolis, the fight over President Trump’s immigration enforcement operations has reached a fever pitch across the country and on Capitol Hill. Some Senate Republicans, however, have said they are open to splitting off the DHS funding from a six-bill package to avoid another shutdown.

Pretti’s killing, as well as the fatal shooting of Renee Good in the same city earlier this month, has called Trump’s position on immigration — which has historically been the president’s strongest issue — into question, as the GOP looks to maintain its majority in this year’s midterm elections.

Trump on Thursday morning will receive an intelligence briefing and host a Cabinet meeting at the White House. He will later join first lady Melania Trump at the premiere of her documentary at the Kennedy Center.

Follow live below.

data.title: Watch live: Homan gives remarks in Minnesota amid turmoil over immigration enforcement
data.description:
Border czar Tom Homan will speak with reporters Thursday morning in Minneapolis, as the city reels following two fatal shootings involving federal immigration authorities.

President Trump sent Homan to Minnesota earlier this week to oversee the administration’s ramped up immigration crackdown in the city after Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino left the area on Tuesday.

The border czar has also met with Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D), which he called “productive.”

Homan’s comments come as the Department of Homeland Security and DHS Secretary Kristi Noem face mounting criticism over its tactics in the North Star State and beyond — with some calling for the agency chief to resign or face impeachment. Lawmakers have also battled over funding for the agency as a partial government shutdown looms.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 8 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.
